text: A small stab wound is made over the inferior crease of the umbilicus in preparation for pediatric laparoscopic appendectomy.
A small stab wound is made over the inferior crease of the umbilicus in preparation for pediatric laparoscopic appendectomy.

The surgeon lifts the abdominal wall and inserts the varus needle, leaving four centimeters exposed. The irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests are performed to ensure proper placement. The insufflator is set to pediatric mode, with gas flow adjusted to 0.1 liters per year of age, causing visible abdominal distension.

The surgeon lifts the abdominal wall and inserts the varus needle, leaving four centimeters exposed. The irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests are performed to ensure proper placement. The insufflator is set to pediatric mode, with gas flow adjusted to 0.1 liters per year of age, and abdominal distension begins as the insufflator starts working.

The surgeon holds the abdominal wall and inserts the varus needle obliquely, monitoring the insufflator's pressure readings as the preset and actual pressures converge at ten. After completing insufflation, the varus needle is removed, and the trocar is carefully introduced with the index finger guarding its tip.

The surgeon holds the abdominal wall and varus needle obliquely while monitoring the insufflator readings. The preset pressure is set to ten, and the actual pressure gradually approaches this value. Once insufflation is complete, the varus needle is removed, and the trocar is carefully introduced with the index finger guarding its tip.

The surgeon introduces the first umbilical port and inserts the telescope, revealing a lump formation in the right iliac fossa with phlegmon and adhered omentum. Gentle blunt dissection is performed to separate the appendix from the abdominal wall, removing the adhesions. The appendix and adhered omentum are further separated using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon introduces the first umbilical port and inserts the telescope, revealing a lump formation in the right iliac fossa with phlegmon and adhered omentum. Gentle blunt dissection is performed to separate the appendix from the abdominal wall, removing the adhesions. The appendix and the adhered omentum are then separated using a harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon uses a 5mm port for the harmonic scalpel to dissect the major appendix, while freeing the omentum. A 3mm pediatric instrument is used through the contralateral port.
The surgeon uses a 5mm port for the harmonic scalpel to dissect the major appendix, while freeing the omentum. A 3mm contralateral port is utilized for pediatric instruments.
The surgeon separates the omental adhesions from the appendix, carefully lifting the omentum to prevent contact with the surrounding bowel loops.
The surgeon separates the omental adhesions from the appendix, carefully lifting the omentum to prevent contact with the surrounding bowel loops.

The surgeon performs skeletonization of the base of the pediatric appendix, carefully dissecting towards the cecum near the tinea coli using a harmonic scalpel. The teflon jaw is positioned towards the appendix to prevent perforation, reaching the base of the appendix near the ileocecal junction.

The surgeon performs skeletonization of the base of the appendix in a pediatric patient, aiming to reach the cecum near the tinea coli. The harmonic scalpel is used with the Teflon jaw positioned towards the appendix to avoid perforation. The base of the appendix is nearly reached, with the ileocecal junction visible below.
The surgeon addresses minor oozing around the surgical site by using suction and irrigation. Mishra's knot, an extracorporeal slip knot, is applied to tie off the appendix.
The surgeon addresses minor oozing around the surgical site by using suction and irrigation. Mishra's knot, an extracorporeal slip knot, is then used to tie off the appendix.

The surgeon uses an extra corporeal Mishra's knot to ligate the appendix, employing a Vandarkar reusable knot pusher with a silicon tip to prevent tissue crushing and suture breakage. The knot pusher is positioned at the base of the appendix, and after ligation, it is removed and the suture is cut.

The surgeon uses an extra corporeal Mishra's knot with a configuration of one hitch, one wind, and one lock repeated three times to ligate the base of the appendix. A darker reusable knot pusher with a silicon tip is employed to secure the knot without crushing the tissue or breaking the suture. The knot pusher is positioned at the base of the appendix, and after ligation, it is removed, and the suture is cut.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the appendix approximately five millimeters away from the knot, effectively burning the mucosa and sealing the distal end to prevent contamination. A 5mm telescope is positioned in the left iliac fossa, and a 10mm umbilical port is used to introduce an endo bag into the abdomen. The endo bag is opened to prepare for specimen retrieval.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the appendix approximately five millimeters away from the previously placed knot. The harmonic seals the distal end of the appendix, preventing contamination by burning the mucosa.

A 5mm telescope is positioned in the left iliac fossa, and a 10mm umbilical port is used to introduce an endo bag into the abdomen. The endo bag is opened, and the appendix is placed inside. The appendix is then extracted through the umbilical port, which is subsequently closed using a cobbler's needle to suture all layers except the skin.

The surgeon unfolds the endo bag and inserts an instrument to create space inside it. The appendix is then placed into the endo bag and extracted through the umbilical port. The umbilical port is closed using a cobbler's needle, ensuring all layers except the skin are sutured.
